stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 7-week-old previously well term male infant brought in by parents with 24 hours of fever, irritability, and reduced feeding (taking about half his usual volume). He has had no vomiting or diarrhoea. There is no rash, no recent travel, and immunisations are up to date. ED has obtained a catheter urine specimen which is positive for nitrites and leukocyte esterase. Bloods are back.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, HR 178, RR 52, SpO2 98% RA, cap refill 2 s
data_table.Examination: Alert when held; wet nappies reduced; no rash; chest clear; abdomen soft, no mass; suprapubic non-tender
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.4, neutrophils 13.2, Hb 110, CRP 84, Na 138, K 4.6, Cr 28; urine: nitrites +, leuk +++, blood +, microscopy WCC >100/mcL; blood and urine cultures sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days with GP review within 48 hours arranged
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient management are inappropriate for febrile UTI under 3 months of age; the bacteraemia risk is too high. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Admit for IV gentamicin and IV benzylpenicillin with renal tract ultrasound during admission
options.rationale: Infants under 3 months with febrile UTI require admission and parenteral antibiotics; empirical IV gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin) is standard, and renal tract ultrasound is recommended during first febrile UTI in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily and arrange micturating cystourethrogram during this admission
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is reserved for late-onset neonatal sepsis or proven resistant organisms; MCUG is not first-line imaging after a first febrile UTI under current Australian practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg three times daily with paediatric review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Oral antibiotic monotherapy is not acceptable for febrile UTI under 3 months irrespective of agent due to bacteraemia and meningitis risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Discharge home awaiting urine culture sensitivities and start antibiotics if a pathogen grows
options.rationale: Awaiting cultures before treating any febrile infant under 3 months with positive urine dipstick risks progression to urosepsis and meningitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Febrile UTI in infants under 3 months is treated as serious bacterial infection until proven otherwise: admission, parenteral antibiotics, blood and urine cultures, and consideration of lumbar puncture if clinically unwell or under 4 weeks. Empirical IV gentamicin plus benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin) is the standard Australian regimen, with oral switch when stable and afebrile typically after 2 to 4 days. Renal tract ultrasound is recommended after a first febrile UTI under 6 months to identify obstruction or significant abnormality. MCUG is reserved for recurrent UTI, atypical organism, or abnormal ultrasound. DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months assesses cortical scarring when indicated. E. coli accounts for over 80% of paediatric UTIs.
key_points: Under 3 months febrile UTI: admit, IV antibiotics, blood + urine cultures
Empirical IV gentamicin + benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin
Renal tract ultrasound after first febrile UTI under 6 months
MCUG only if recurrent, atypical, or abnormal USS
Catheter or SPA urine specimen preferred under 2 years
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin once-daily at 7.5 mg/kg in neonates and infants is preferred to divided dosing in Australian paediatric practice because of superior peak-to-MIC bactericidal activity and reduced nephrotoxicity.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 2-year-old girl in ED with a 1-day history of barking cough and stridor. She had a coryzal prodrome 2 days ago. At triage she had inspiratory stridor at rest with mild chest wall retractions, and was given a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg 30 minutes ago. On reassessment she now has inspiratory stridor at rest with marked sternal recession, looks distressed, and is reluctant to drink. She is afebrile.

data_table.Examination: Inspiratory stridor at rest, marked sternal recession, mild tracheal tug, no drooling, no cyanosis, alert but distressed, air entry symmetrical with no wheeze
data_table.Investigations: No bloods or imaging needed at this stage

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg (5 mL of 1:1000) with continued close monitoring in resuscitation bay
options.rationale: Stridor at rest with marked sternal recession indicates severe croup not yet responding to dexamethasone; nebulised adrenaline gives rapid (within 30 minutes) symptomatic relief and is indicated for moderate-severe croup. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Repeat oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg now and reassess clinical severity in a further 60 minutes
options.rationale: Dexamethasone takes 1 to 2 hours for effect; repeating it now does not provide rapid relief and the child needs immediate intervention. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Heated humidified oxygen by mask at 6 L/min and reassess clinical severity in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Cochrane evidence shows no benefit of humidified air for croup; oxygen is given only if SpO2 is low, which is not the case here. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Urgent lateral neck radiograph to exclude foreign body or epiglottitis before further treatment
options.rationale: Imaging is not indicated in classic croup and risks deterioration if the child is distressed; clinical severity scoring guides management. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg via spacer with budesonide and clinical reassessment in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not treat upper airway obstruction; nebulised budesonide is an alternative steroid but oral dexamethasone has already been given. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup severity is graded as mild (stridor only with agitation, no recession), moderate (stridor at rest with mild-moderate recession), severe (stridor at rest with marked recession or distress, or hypoxia or altered mental state). All grades receive oral dexamethasone 0.15 to 0.6 mg/kg (single dose). Moderate to severe croup additionally receives nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 (or 0.5 mL/kg, max 5 mL), which acts within 30 minutes. Adrenaline effect lasts 1 to 2 hours; child must be observed for rebound. Disposition depends on response: home if mild and steroid effect achieved, observation for 4 hours post-adrenaline, admission for ongoing stridor at rest. Avoid distressing the child (no IV, no imaging unless essential).
key_points: Severity: mild (stridor with agitation), moderate (stridor at rest), severe (marked recession, hypoxia)
All grades: oral dexamethasone 0.15-0.6 mg/kg single dose
Moderate-severe: add nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000
Adrenaline acts within 30 minutes, effect 1-2 hours
Avoid distressing investigations
explanation.pearls: Nebulised L-adrenaline (1:1000) at 5 mL is equivalent in efficacy to racemic adrenaline 2.25% at 0.5 mL, and is the standard formulation in Australian paediatric ED; rebound stridor at 1 to 2 hours mandates 4-hour observation post-dose.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar in ED at 02:30 reviewing a previously well 13-year-old boy with sudden onset severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 4 hours ago. He has vomited twice. He denies trauma, dysuria, or recent illness. He has not previously been sexually active. The left testis is high-riding with a horizontal lie and exquisitely tender; the cremasteric reflex is absent on the left. The right side is unremarkable. Urinalysis is clear.

data_table.Examination: Left hemiscrotum erythematous, swollen, left testis high-riding with horizontal lie, exquisite tenderness, absent cremasteric reflex; right side normal; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: nil; FBC and EUC sent
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol 1 g PO 1 hour ago in ED

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound to assess testicular blood flow before any surgical referral
options.rationale: Imaging delays definitive treatment and risks loss of the testis; with classic clinical findings, theatre takes priority over imaging. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

options.text: Empirical oral ceftriaxone and doxycycline for presumed epididymo-orchitis with urology review
options.rationale: Epididymitis usually has gradual onset, positive cremasteric reflex, and abnormal urinalysis; this presentation is classic torsion. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

options.text: Immediate surgical exploration of the scrotum with bilateral orchidopexy by urology this hour
options.rationale: Time from onset to detorsion determines testicular salvage (about 90% salvage if under 6 hours, falling to under 10% by 24 hours); classic clinical features mandate theatre without delay for imaging. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

options.text: Attempt manual detorsion in ED rotating the left testis medially and admit for observation overnight
options.rationale: Manual detorsion (open-book, lateral rotation) may be a bridging measure where surgery is delayed, but does not replace urgent theatre; rotation direction is lateral, not medial, in most cases. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

options.text: Admit under paediatrics for analgesia and elevation, with urology review on the morning round
options.rationale: Delay beyond 6 hours dramatically reduces testicular salvage; deferring urology assessment is a sentinel error. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute scrotum)

explanation.summary: Testicular torsion is a urological emergency where time to detorsion determines salvage. Classic features are sudden severe scrotal pain (often waking from sleep), vomiting, high-riding testis with horizontal lie, and absent cremasteric reflex. Salvage rates are around 90% if surgery occurs within 6 hours, falling to under 10% beyond 24 hours. When clinical suspicion is high, the patient goes directly to theatre; ultrasound is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty and must not delay surgery. Surgical exploration includes detorsion, viability assessment, and bilateral orchidopexy because the underlying bell-clapper deformity is bilateral. Differential includes epididymitis (gradual, urinary symptoms), torsion of appendix testis (blue dot sign, less severe), and incarcerated hernia.
key_points: Salvage ~90% if surgery <6 hours, <10% by 24 hours
Classic: sudden pain, vomiting, high-riding horizontal testis, absent cremasteric reflex
Do not delay theatre for ultrasound when clinical suspicion is high
Bilateral orchidopexy (bell-clapper is bilateral)
Differential: epididymitis (gradual, urinary symptoms), torsion of appendix testis
explanation.pearls: Absent cremasteric reflex has about 99% sensitivity for testicular torsion in adolescent boys, making it the single most useful bedside finding when paired with sudden severe pain.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar admitting a 9-month-old boy with 6 days of fever to 39.5, marked irritability, bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, and a polymorphous truncal rash. He has no cervical lymphadenopathy, no oral changes, and no extremity changes yet. Mucosal swabs and blood cultures are pending. Initial bloods show neutrophilic leukocytosis, raised CRP, mild anaemia, hyponatraemia, low albumin, and elevated ALT. Echocardiogram performed by paediatric cardiology shows borderline left anterior descending coronary dilation (Z score 2.3).

data_table.Examination: Bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection without exudate, polymorphous truncal rash, irritable; no lymphadenopathy, no oral or hand or foot changes
data_table.Investigations: WCC 19, neutrophils 14, Hb 96, plt 540, CRP 162, ESR 78, Na 130, ALT 88, albumin 28; urinalysis: sterile pyuria; echocardiogram: LAD Z score 2.3, no pericardial effusion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue supportive care and antipyretics; reassess at day 10 of fever to apply Kawasaki criteria
options.rationale: Awaiting full criteria delays treatment beyond day 10 and increases coronary aneurysm risk; incomplete Kawasaki with coronary changes is treated as Kawasaki. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for empirical sepsis with paediatric infectious diseases consultation
options.rationale: Septic workup is reasonable but ceftriaxone alone does not address the most likely diagnosis; coronary changes on echo support incomplete Kawasaki. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg daily for 3 days as first-line treatment of incomplete Kawasaki
options.rationale: Pulse methylprednisolone is reserved for IVIG resistance or as IVIG-adjunct in high-risk; it is not first-line in standard practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 12 hours with high-dose oral aspirin 30 mg/kg/day divided
options.rationale: Infants under 1 year with prolonged fever, supportive lab features (raised CRP, sterile pyuria, hyponatraemia, low albumin, raised ALT) and coronary changes meet incomplete Kawasaki criteria; IVIG 2 g/kg plus high-dose aspirin within 10 days reduces coronary aneurysm risk from about 25% to under 5%. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease; AHA Kawasaki Statement)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily monotherapy and repeat echocardiogram in 7 days for review
options.rationale: Oral steroid monotherapy is not standard initial therapy; IVIG remains the first-line agent with proven coronary protection. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

explanation.summary: Classic Kawasaki disease requires fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 criteria (bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, oral mucosal changes, polymorphous rash, peripheral extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy). Incomplete Kawasaki applies when fewer criteria are met, especially in infants under 1 year and children over 8 (highest aneurysm risk groups). The AHA algorithm allows treatment when fever and 2 to 3 criteria coexist with elevated CRP/ESR plus supportive features (anaemia, thrombocytosis after day 7, raised ALT, hypoalbuminaemia, leukocytosis, sterile pyuria) OR coronary abnormalities on echo (Z score >=2.5 or dilation). IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion plus high-dose aspirin within 10 days of fever onset reduces coronary aneurysm risk from 25% to under 5%. Aspirin is reduced to antiplatelet dose after fever resolution.
key_points: Classic: fever >=5 days + 4 of 5 (CRASH and burn - Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue, Hands/feet)
Incomplete: fewer criteria but suggestive labs or coronary findings
IVIG 2 g/kg + high-dose aspirin within 10 days reduces aneurysm rate 25% to <5%
Infants <1 year highest aneurysm risk - lower threshold to treat
Echo at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6 to 8 weeks
explanation.pearls: Coronary artery Z score >=2.5 on echocardiogram in a febrile child with under 4 classic criteria is sufficient to diagnose incomplete Kawasaki and commit to IVIG, regardless of inflammatory markers.

stem: You are the neonatal registrar called to postnatal ward at 90 minutes of life to review a term male infant born by elective LSCS to a 32-year-old G2P1 with insulin-treated type 1 diabetes. Birth weight is 4350 g (>97th centile). Maternal HbA1c at 36 weeks was 7.4%. The infant was placed skin-to-skin and breastfed at 30 minutes. Routine BSL screening at 90 minutes is 1.6 mmol/L (taken before the second feed). The infant is alert with normal tone, no jitteriness, no apnoea, and a normal cry.

data_table.Examination: Term appearance, large for gestational age, alert, normal tone, no jitteriness, no apnoea, plethoric but not cyanotic
data_table.Investigations: Bedside BSL 1.6 mmol/L; cord gases at delivery normal; no further bloods yet
data_table.Medications: Vitamin K 1 mg IM at birth

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue breastfeeding only and recheck BSL prior to the next scheduled feed in 3 hours
options.rationale: BSL 1.6 in an at-risk asymptomatic infant requires active intervention; awaiting another feed risks symptomatic hypoglycaemia and neurological injury. (Source: ANZCOR Neonatal Resuscitation; RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Give a single bolus of 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg intravenously and continue routine ward observations afterwards
options.rationale: IV bolus dextrose is reserved for symptomatic hypoglycaemia or BSL persistently <1.2 despite feeds and gel; first-line in asymptomatic at-risk infant is dextrose gel. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Commence intravenous 10% dextrose at 60 mL/kg/day infusion via cannula and admit to neonatal nursery
options.rationale: IV dextrose is reserved for symptomatic, severe (<1.2), or refractory hypoglycaemia after dextrose gel and feed; this asymptomatic infant should have buccal gel first. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Administer intramuscular glucagon 100 mcg/kg single dose with continued breastfeeding and observation
options.rationale: Glucagon is reserved for severe persistent hypoglycaemia where IV access is delayed; infants of diabetic mothers have depleted glycogen stores and respond unreliably. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

options.text: Administer 40% buccal dextrose gel 200 mg/kg with immediate top-up feed and recheck BSL in 30 minutes
options.rationale: For asymptomatic at-risk neonatal hypoglycaemia in the first 48 hours, 40% buccal dextrose gel 200 mg/kg (0.5 mL/kg) plus feed is the preferred Australian first-line intervention, with BSL recheck in 30 minutes; this avoids separation and IV access. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia; ANZNN)

explanation.summary: Infants at risk of neonatal hypoglycaemia (LGA, IDM, SGA, preterm) are screened with bedside BSL from 90 minutes of life and before feeds for 12 to 24 hours. Operational threshold for action is BSL <2.6 mmol/L in the first 48 hours. For asymptomatic at-risk infants, 40% dextrose gel 200 mg/kg (0.5 mL/kg) massaged into the buccal mucosa followed by feed is first-line; this Australian Sugar Babies protocol increases breastfeeding success and reduces NICU admissions. Recheck BSL at 30 minutes. IV 10% dextrose at 60 to 80 mL/kg/day is reserved for symptomatic infants, BSL <1.2, or BSL not responsive to gel and feeds. Symptomatic infants (jitteriness, seizures, apnoea, lethargy) require immediate IV dextrose 2 mL/kg of 10% then maintenance infusion. Critical sample (insulin, cortisol, growth hormone, ammonia, lactate) is taken before treatment when persistent hypoglycaemia beyond 48 hours raises concern for hyperinsulinism.
key_points: Operational threshold BSL <2.6 in first 48 hours for at-risk infants
Asymptomatic: 40% dextrose gel 200 mg/kg + feed, recheck 30 min
Symptomatic or BSL <1.2: IV 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg bolus + infusion
Critical sample if persistent hypoglycaemia beyond 48 hours
IDM: hyperinsulinism, depleted glycogen, vulnerable >48 hours
explanation.pearls: The Sugar Babies trial showed buccal 40% dextrose gel 200 mg/kg reduced NICU admission for neonatal hypoglycaemia by approximately 50% compared with feeds alone, and is the basis for Australian first-line use.

stem: You are the paediatric nephrology registrar reviewing an 8-year-old boy 6 weeks after a clinical diagnosis of Henoch-Schönlein purpura (palpable purpura, polyarthralgia, mild abdominal pain) which has otherwise resolved. He was managed conservatively at first presentation. Today his palpable purpura has cleared. Routine urinalysis shows persistent proteinuria and microscopic haematuria. Spot urine protein:creatinine ratio is 280 mg/mmol (heavily nephrotic range). Serum albumin is 22 g/L. eGFR is 78 mL/min/1.73m2 (mildly reduced). He is normotensive. Complement and ANA are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/64 (50th centile for age and height), HR 88
data_table.Examination: Mild periorbital and ankle oedema, purpura resolved, abdomen soft, joints non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: protein +++, blood ++; UPCR 280 mg/mmol; albumin 22 g/L; eGFR 78; C3 normal, C4 normal, ANA negative, anti-dsDNA negative; hep B/C and ASOT negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure and recheck urinalysis and renal function in 6 months as HSP nephritis usually self-resolves
options.rationale: Nephrotic-range proteinuria with hypoalbuminaemia carries a high risk of progression and demands biopsy to grade severity, not watchful waiting. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases; KHA-CARI)

options.text: Empirical oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily for 4 weeks then taper, without histological confirmation now
options.rationale: Empirical steroids without histology in nephrotic HSP may undertreat crescentic disease and overtreat mild lesions; biopsy guides immunosuppression intensity. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases)

options.text: Renal biopsy now to grade nephritis severity and guide immunosuppressive therapy choice
options.rationale: HSP nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypoalbuminaemia, or impaired GFR persisting beyond 4 weeks is an indication for renal biopsy to assess for crescents (ISKDC grading) and direct therapy intensity; isolated microscopic haematuria does not require biopsy. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases; RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Commence ACE inhibitor monotherapy (enalapril) with monthly urinalysis and BP monitoring at GP
options.rationale: ACE inhibitor is appropriate adjunct for ongoing proteinuria but does not replace biopsy and immunosuppression decision in nephrotic-range disease. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases)

options.text: Plasmapheresis with IV pulse methylprednisolone now and admit under nephrology for cyclophosphamide
options.rationale: Plasmapheresis and cyclophosphamide are reserved for biopsy-proven crescentic disease (typically >50% crescents) with rapidly progressive renal failure; this stable presentation requires histology first. (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases)

explanation.summary: HSP nephritis affects 20 to 50% of children with HSP and is the main determinant of long-term outcome. Most cases (isolated microscopic haematuria or low-grade proteinuria) resolve without specific treatment, but persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria, hypoalbuminaemia, hypertension, or impaired GFR carry significant risk of progression to chronic kidney disease (about 5 to 10% develop ESKD over 20 years). Renal biopsy is indicated for nephrotic syndrome, persistent proteinuria after 4 weeks of UPCR >50 mg/mmol, raised creatinine, or hypertension. ISKDC grading by percentage crescents drives therapy: low grade (no crescents) receives ACE inhibitor; moderate (under 50%) receives oral prednisolone; severe (over 50% crescents) requires IV methylprednisolone, cyclophosphamide or MMF, and plasmapheresis in selected cases. All children with HSP need urinalysis surveillance for 6 months minimum.
key_points: HSP nephritis: main determinant of long-term outcome
Biopsy indication: nephrotic, persistent UPCR >50, raised Cr, HTN
ISKDC grade by crescents directs therapy intensity
ACE inhibitor adjunctively for proteinuria reduction
Surveillance urinalysis 6 months minimum after HSP
explanation.pearls: Crescent involvement of >50% of glomeruli on biopsy (ISKDC grade IV to V) in HSP nephritis carries about 25% risk of ESKD by 20 years and triggers aggressive immunosuppression including pulse methylprednisolone and cyclophosphamide.

stem: You are the paediatric oncology registrar reviewing a 3-year-old girl with a 3-week history of progressive abdominal distension, intermittent low-grade fever, weight loss, and bone pain in both legs causing limping. Examination shows a 10 cm firm abdominal mass crossing the midline. She has periorbital ecchymosis bilaterally. There is no haematuria. BP is 90th centile for age. Urinary VMA and HVA are markedly elevated. MIBG scan is being arranged. Bone marrow aspirate is being arranged.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68 (90th centile), HR 124, T 37.8, RR 24, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: 10 cm firm irregular abdominal mass crossing midline, bilateral periorbital ecchymosis (raccoon eyes), opsoclonus on prolonged ocular pursuit, tender shins bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: Hb 84, plt 198, WCC 12, LDH 1240, urinary VMA and HVA markedly elevated; MRI abdomen: 10 cm adrenal mass crossing midline encasing aorta

lead_in: Which feature most strongly differentiates this presentation from Wilms tumour?

options.text: Presence of a firm palpable abdominal mass detected on routine paediatric abdominal examination today
options.rationale: Abdominal mass is the classical presentation of BOTH Wilms tumour and neuroblastoma; it does not discriminate between them on its own. (Source: COG Neuroblastoma Risk Stratification)

options.text: Hypertension within the 90th centile range for age detected on routine ward observations today
options.rationale: Both Wilms (renin) and neuroblastoma (catecholamines) can cause hypertension; this feature alone does not discriminate. (Source: COG Neuroblastoma Risk Stratification)

options.text: Mass crossing the abdominal midline with encasement of the aorta on the most recent MRI scan
options.rationale: Crossing the midline favours neuroblastoma but is not absolutely specific; vascular encasement also occurs in advanced Wilms. (Source: COG Neuroblastoma Risk Stratification)

options.text: Bilateral periorbital ecchymosis (raccoon eyes) with opsoclonus and bone pain from skeletal metastasis
options.rationale: Periorbital ecchymosis from orbital metastases, opsoclonus-myoclonus paraneoplastic syndrome, and lytic bone metastases are hallmark neuroblastoma features that do not occur with Wilms; metastasis to bone, marrow, orbit, and skin is typical of neuroblastoma stage M. (Source: COG Neuroblastoma Risk Stratification)

options.text: Markedly elevated urinary catecholamine metabolite levels (VMA and HVA) detected on urine testing
options.rationale: Urinary VMA and HVA are highly specific for neuroblastoma but the stem question asks about clinical features that differentiate; the orbital and bone disease pattern is more clinically discriminating. (Source: COG Neuroblastoma Risk Stratification)

explanation.summary: Neuroblastoma and Wilms tumour are the two commonest paediatric solid abdominal malignancies. Neuroblastoma (median age 2 years) arises from neural crest, typically adrenal but can be any sympathetic chain site; it crosses midline, encases vessels, and metastasises to bone marrow, cortical bone, orbits, skin, and liver, with pathognomonic raccoon eyes, opsoclonus-myoclonus, and Horner syndrome. Wilms tumour (median age 3 to 4) arises from kidney, displaces structures, rarely crosses midline, and metastasises to lung and liver; associated with WAGR, Beckwith-Wiedemann, and Denys-Drash syndromes. Urinary VMA and HVA are elevated in over 90% of neuroblastomas; staging includes MIBG and bilateral bone marrow biopsy; INRG risk stratification (age, MYCN amplification, ploidy, histology, stage) drives therapy intensity from observation through surgery, chemotherapy, autologous SCT, and anti-GD2 immunotherapy.
key_points: Neuroblastoma: neural crest, crosses midline, encases vessels, bone/marrow/orbit metastases
Wilms: renal, displaces, rarely crosses midline, lung/liver metastases
Neuroblastoma paraneoplastic: opsoclonus-myoclonus, racoon eyes, Horner
Urinary VMA/HVA elevated in >90% of neuroblastomas
INRG risk stratification drives treatment intensity
explanation.pearls: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome occurs in 2 to 3% of children with neuroblastoma, often signalling lower-stage tumour with paradoxically worse neurological outcome; immunotherapy with rituximab is increasingly used for the paraneoplastic component.

stem: You are the paediatric immunology registrar reviewing a 4-year-old boy referred by GP with 8 documented episodes over the past 6 months of fever to 40, aphthous stomatitis, pharyngitis with negative throat swabs, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Episodes last 4 days at a time and recur every 3 to 4 weeks with clockwork regularity. Between episodes he is entirely well. Growth is on the 50th centile. Family history is negative for periodic fevers. Genetic testing for MEFV, TNFRSF1A, MVK, and NLRP3 has returned negative.

data_table.Examination: During episode: aphthous stomatitis, exudative pharyngitis, tender cervical lymphadenopathy; between episodes: examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: During episode: CRP 120, neutrophilia, normal ferritin; between episodes: CRP <5, normal FBC; throat swabs negative on all episodes; genetic panel for FMF (MEFV), TRAPS (TNFRSF1A), HIDS (MVK), CAPS (NLRP3) all negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Empirical daily oral colchicine 0.5 mg twice daily as prophylaxis against further episodes of fever
options.rationale: Colchicine is the treatment for familial Mediterranean fever which has been excluded genetically; PFAPA responds variably to colchicine but it is not first-line. (Source: Eurofever PFAPA Guideline)

options.text: Continuous oral cimetidine 20 mg/kg/day for 6 months as prophylaxis against further febrile attacks
options.rationale: Cimetidine has small case-series support for PFAPA prophylaxis but is not first-line; steroids at onset have the most robust evidence and immediate effect. (Source: Eurofever PFAPA Guideline)

options.text: Subcutaneous anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist) 2 mg/kg daily during each acute febrile episode now
options.rationale: Anakinra is reserved for refractory PFAPA or biopsy-proven monogenic syndromes (CAPS, HIDS); it is not first-line and is not appropriate before trial of oral steroids. (Source: Eurofever PFAPA Guideline)

options.text: Urgent referral to ENT for tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy as definitive treatment of recurrent fever
options.rationale: Tonsillectomy is curative in 70 to 90% but is reserved for steroid-responsive PFAPA refractory to medical therapy or significantly affecting quality of life; trial of steroids comes first. (Source: Eurofever PFAPA Guideline)

options.text: Single oral dose of prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg at fever onset to abort each PFAPA episode promptly
options.rationale: PFAPA (Periodic Fever, Aphthous stomatitis, Pharyngitis, cervical Adenitis) responds dramatically to a single oral steroid dose at fever onset (response within hours); this is both diagnostic and therapeutic; first-line per Eurofever and Australian paediatric immunology practice. (Source: Eurofever PFAPA Guideline)

explanation.summary: PFAPA (Periodic Fever, Aphthous stomatitis, Pharyngitis, cervical Adenitis) is the commonest paediatric autoinflammatory syndrome, presenting between ages 2 and 5 with clockwork-regular febrile episodes lasting 3 to 6 days at 3 to 8 week intervals. Diagnosis is clinical after exclusion of monogenic periodic fevers (FMF, TRAPS, HIDS, CAPS) by genetic testing when family history suggests, and after exclusion of cyclic neutropenia by serial neutrophil counts. Pathognomonic feature is dramatic and rapid response to a single oral dose of prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg at fever onset, which aborts the episode within hours but may shorten intervals between episodes. Other options include cimetidine prophylaxis, colchicine, anakinra (refractory cases), or tonsillectomy (70 to 90% remission). Children grow out of PFAPA by adolescence in most cases. PFAPA does not cause amyloidosis or organ damage, unlike untreated FMF.
key_points: PFAPA: commonest paediatric autoinflammatory, ages 2-5
Clockwork fevers, exclude monogenic syndromes (FMF, TRAPS, HIDS, CAPS) genetically
Single oral prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg aborts episode within hours
Tonsillectomy curative in 70-90% if refractory
Resolves by adolescence in most cases without sequelae
explanation.pearls: Steroid response in PFAPA is so brisk and reproducible that resolution of fever within 4 hours of a single oral prednisolone 1 to 2 mg/kg dose is considered confirmatory of the diagnosis when other periodic fever syndromes have been excluded.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 28-year-old G1P0 at 33+2 weeks gestation, referred from antenatal clinic with new hypertension. Her booking BP was 110/68. Today repeated BP measurements are 152/98 and 148/94 on the contralateral arm after 15 minutes rest. She is asymptomatic with no headache, no visual disturbance, no epigastric pain, and no ankle swelling beyond mild dependent oedema. Foetal movements are normal and recent growth scan was on the 40th centile. Routine bloods and urinalysis have been sent.

data_table.Examination: No epigastric tenderness, no clonus, no facial oedema, mild ankle oedema bilaterally, gravid uterus consistent with dates, foetal heart 142
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: protein nil, blood nil; PCR 8 mg/mmol (normal <30); Hb 118, plt 244, ALT 24, AST 22, creatinine 64; uric acid 0.34; CTG: reassuring

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge with twice weekly community midwifery BP monitoring and review at the antenatal clinic in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Severe-range hypertension (>=160/110) is an admission threshold but 148-152/94-98 (non-severe) still warrants careful evaluation; community monitoring alone misses progression to pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Admit for IV magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis and urgent delivery by induction of labour within 24 hours
options.rationale: Magnesium prophylaxis and expedited delivery are reserved for severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia; gestational hypertension without proteinuria or features does not meet either threshold. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence oral labetalol 100 mg twice daily and arrange day assessment unit review twice weekly
options.rationale: New non-severe hypertension in pregnancy (BP 140 to 159/90 to 109) without proteinuria or end-organ involvement is gestational hypertension; labetalol is the Australian first-line, with day-stay or DAU monitoring twice weekly for progression to pre-eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Start oral methyldopa 250 mg three times daily and arrange routine antenatal follow-up in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Methyldopa is acceptable but labetalol is preferred first-line per SOMANZ; the major error is the 4-week interval which is far too long for new hypertension. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Reassure as white-coat hypertension and provide a home BP monitor for daily readings over 7 days
options.rationale: Repeated readings on contralateral arms in clinical setting confirm true hypertension; home monitoring alone misses end-organ involvement evolution. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: SOMANZ defines gestational hypertension as new-onset BP >=140/90 after 20 weeks without proteinuria or end-organ involvement. Pre-eclampsia adds proteinuria (PCR >=30 mg/mmol or ACR >=8) or end-organ dysfunction (renal, liver, neurological, haematological, uteroplacental). Severe hypertension is >=160/110 and requires admission and acute lowering with IV labetalol, oral nifedipine, or IV hydralazine. Non-severe hypertension (140 to 159/90 to 109) is treated with oral labetalol (Australian first-line), oral nifedipine, or methyldopa; target BP is 110 to 140 systolic and 80 to 95 diastolic. Twice-weekly DAU monitoring includes BP, urinalysis, FBC, EUC, LFTs, PCR, CTG, and growth scans every 2 to 4 weeks. Aspirin (started before 16 weeks) prevents 60% of pre-eclampsia in high-risk women. Delivery timing depends on severity: 37 weeks for gestational hypertension or non-severe pre-eclampsia, earlier for severe disease.
key_points: Gestational HTN: BP >=140/90, no proteinuria, no end-organ involvement
Pre-eclampsia: + proteinuria (PCR >=30) OR end-organ dysfunction
Severe HTN >=160/110: admit + acute lowering
Non-severe HTN: oral labetalol first-line + DAU surveillance twice weekly
explanation.pearls: About 25% of women with gestational hypertension progress to pre-eclampsia within 2 to 4 weeks, which is why twice-weekly surveillance with urinalysis and bloods is mandatory until delivery.

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar reviewing a 32-year-old G2P1 at 37+5 weeks gestation in established labour (4 cm dilated, ROM 6 hours ago). Her booking notes show vaginal-rectal GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. Her first child was born vaginally 3 years ago at another hospital without complication; that baby had no neonatal infection. Today she is afebrile, has been on no antibiotics, and has had no urinary GBS at any time this pregnancy. Foetal heart rate is reassuring.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm, fully effaced, vertex at 0 station, intact membranes ruptured 6 hours ago with clear liquor; uterus relaxed between contractions
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36 weeks: negative; urinalysis: clear; FBC and group and hold valid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum management?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g 4 hourly throughout labour as routine GBS prophylaxis
options.rationale: Negative GBS swab at 36 weeks (within 5 weeks of delivery), no GBS bacteriuria, no previous GBS-infected infant, term and afebrile means no GBS prophylaxis is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG GBS in Pregnancy)

options.text: Repeat vaginal-rectal GBS swab now and start IV benzylpenicillin pending results during labour
options.rationale: Repeating swab in labour does not change immediate decisions and starting prophylaxis pending unnecessary results is overtreatment. (Source: RANZCOG GBS in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g 8 hourly throughout labour as routine GBS prophylaxis given the duration of ROM
options.rationale: Duration of ROM <18 hours at term with negative GBS swab is not an indication for prophylaxis; cefazolin would be reserved for penicillin allergy with low anaphylaxis risk. (Source: RANZCOG GBS in Pregnancy)

options.text: Routine labour care with continuous CTG monitoring and no intrapartum antibiotic for GBS now
options.rationale: Negative GBS swab within 5 weeks of delivery in low-risk labour (afebrile, term, no prior GBS infant, no GBS bacteriuria) excludes need for prophylaxis under universal-screening RANZCOG/SOMANZ pathway. (Source: RANZCOG GBS in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 900 mg 8 hourly throughout labour as routine GBS prophylaxis cover
options.rationale: Clindamycin is for penicillin-allergic women with high anaphylaxis risk AND positive GBS or risk factors; no indication here. (Source: RANZCOG GBS in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Australian RANZCOG guidelines recommend universal antenatal vaginal-rectal GBS screening at 35 to 37 weeks with intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) for: positive swab within 5 weeks, GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy, previous GBS-infected infant, preterm labour <37 weeks (regardless of swab status), or intrapartum fever >=38 (regardless of swab status). First-line IAP is IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g 4-hourly; cefazolin for low-risk penicillin allergy; clindamycin (if sensitive) or vancomycin for high-risk allergy. Optimum coverage requires at least 4 hours before delivery, but partial cover is still given. PROM at term is not in itself an indication for IAP if GBS swab is negative within 5 weeks. Newborn observation is required for at least 24 to 48 hours in IAP exposure; full sepsis workup for unwell neonates.
key_points: Universal screening at 35-37 weeks (RANZCOG approach)
IAP indications: positive swab, GBS bacteriuria, prior GBS-infected infant, preterm, intrapartum fever
First-line: IV benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g 4 hourly
Optimum coverage requires >=4 hours before delivery
Negative swab + low risk = NO antibiotic needed
explanation.pearls: Intrapartum benzylpenicillin given >=4 hours before delivery reduces early-onset neonatal GBS disease by approximately 86%; partial coverage (less than 4 hours) still provides about 47% reduction.
